question: What is a cell?
answer: the building block of all living things
accept: the basic unit of a living thing
what all living things are made of

question: Name the four sub-cellular structures found in all cell types.
answer: cell membrane
cytoplasm
ribosomes
genetic material
misconceptions: nucleus is found in all cells
chloroplast is found in all cells
mitochondria are found in all cells
cell wall is found in all cells

question: Which sub-cellular structure controls what substances enter and leave the cell?
answer: Cell membrane
options: Cell membrane
Cytoplasm
Ribosome
Genetic material
distractors: the cytoplasm is where chemical reactions happen, not where substances are controlled in and out
the ribosome is where proteins are made, not a barrier
genetic material carries instructions, it does not control movement of substances
misconceptions: the cell wall controls what enters

question: State the function of the cytoplasm.
answer: where the cell's chemical reactions happen
misconceptions: it is empty space inside the cell

question: What is the function of the ribosomes?
answer: where proteins are made

question: Which two sub-cellular structures are needed to make proteins, and why are both needed?
answer: genetic material and ribosomes
the genetic material provides the instructions to make the proteins
and the ribosomes are where the proteins are actually made
accept: ribosomes are the site of protein synthesis

question: A pupil says "all cells have a cell wall". Why is this wrong?
answer: Only plant and bacterial cells have a cell wall; animal cells do not
options: Only plant and bacterial cells have a cell wall; animal cells do not
No cells have a cell wall
Only animal cells have a cell wall
The cell wall is the same thing as the cell membrane
distractors: plant and bacterial cells do have cell walls, so saying no cells have one is wrong
it is animal cells that lack a cell wall, not plant cells that have it
the cell wall and cell membrane are different structures with different jobs
misconceptions: all cells have a cell wall
animal cells have a cell wall
the cell wall and cell membrane are the same

question: Oxygen needs to get into a cell to be used. Explain which structure it passes through and why.
answer: it passes through the cell membrane
because the cell membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell
accept: membrane allows substances to move in and out
misconceptions: it passes through the cell wall
it passes through the cytoplasm directly from outside

question: Why can the microscope used in this unit not be used to see ribosomes or genetic material?
answer: These structures are too small to be seen with this microscope
options: These structures are too small to be seen with this microscope
These structures are invisible because they are colourless
Only plant cells contain these structures
These structures only appear when the cell is dead
distractors: the issue is their size, not their colour
ribosomes and genetic material are found in all cell types, not just plants
the structures are present in living cells; size is the limitation
misconceptions: all sub-cellular structures can be seen with a microscope
ribosomes are large enough to see
a microscope can show every part of a cell

question: A microscope does more than just zoom in. Describe the two things it does to an image.
answer: it magnifies the image to make it bigger
and it focuses the image to make it sharp
accept: makes a bigger image for magnifies
misconceptions: it only makes the image bigger

question: A cell has an actual size of 0.05 mm. It is viewed at a magnification of 100. What is the size of the image?
answer: 5 mm
working: image size = actual size × magnification = 0.05 × 100 = 5 mm

question: What is a unicellular organism?
answer: an organism made of only one cell
accept: an organism that is just one cell
misconceptions: an organism made of many cells

question: Give one example of a unicellular organism.
answer: bacteria

question: Explain the difference between a unicellular organism and a multicellular organism.
answer: a unicellular organism is made of only one cell
a multicellular organism is made of many cells working together
misconceptions: unicellular means a plant and multicellular means an animal
unicellular organisms have one nucleus and multicellular have many
they are the plant and animal kingdoms

question: What is the function of the flagellum on a bacterial cell?
answer: it allows the bacteria to move
accept: helps the bacterium move from place to place

question: Why does a bacterium need a flagellum?
answer: so it can move from one place to another
to find sources of food needed for survival

question: What is the function of the slime capsule found around some bacterial cells?
answer: it protects the cell
accept: protects the cell from disease or damage

question: Which two structures are found in a plant cell but are NOT found in a bacterial cell?
answer: Nucleus and mitochondria
options: Nucleus and mitochondria
Cell wall and ribosomes
Cytoplasm and cell membrane
Genetic material and cell wall
distractors: bacterial cells do have a cell wall and ribosomes
bacterial cells do have cytoplasm and a cell membrane
bacterial cells contain genetic material and a cell wall
misconceptions: bacterial cells have no cell wall

question: Explain why a bacterial cell does not have mitochondria.
answer: bacterial cells are very, very small
a bacterial cell is about the same size as a single mitochondrion, so a mitochondrion could not fit inside
accept: the mitochondrion is too big to fit in such a small cell
misconceptions: mitochondria are only found in plants

question: Why does a bacterial cell not have a nucleus?
answer: A nucleus would take up too much space in such a small cell
options: A nucleus would take up too much space in such a small cell
Bacteria have no genetic material to put in a nucleus
A nucleus would stop the bacterium moving
Only dead cells have a nucleus
distractors: bacteria do have genetic material; it just is not surrounded by a nucleus
the flagellum, not the nucleus, is linked to movement
living cells can have a nucleus; size is the real reason here
misconceptions: bacterial genetic material floats free because the cell is dead

question: A yeast cell has many of the structures of a plant cell but no chloroplasts. Explain why.
answer: yeast cannot make their own food using sunlight
so they do not need chloroplasts
misconceptions: yeast make food from sunlight like plants
yeast have chloroplasts but they are hidden

question: A bacterial cell has an actual size of 0.002 mm. Under a microscope its image is magnified 500 times. What is the size of the image?
answer: 1 mm
working: image size = actual size × magnification = 0.002 × 500 = 1 mm

question: What is a specialised cell?
answer: a cell shaped or adapted to do one job really well
accept: a cell adapted for a particular function
misconceptions: a cell that does every job
a cell that is bigger than normal

question: What is the function of a red blood cell?
answer: to carry oxygen

question: What is the name of the shape of a red blood cell?
answer: biconcave

question: Why does a red blood cell have no nucleus?
answer: So there is more space to carry oxygen
options: So there is more space to carry oxygen
So it can move on its own
So it can photosynthesise
So it can make more proteins
distractors: red blood cells do not move themselves; they are carried in the blood
red blood cells do not photosynthesise
losing the nucleus is about oxygen space, not making proteins
misconceptions: the missing nucleus increases surface area
red blood cells never had a nucleus and so cannot work
the nucleus is removed to help it move

question: Explain how the biconcave shape of a red blood cell helps it do its job.
answer: the shape allows it to fit through small blood vessels
and it increases the surface area, so more oxygen can be carried
accept: bigger surface area for absorbing oxygen
misconceptions: the biconcave shape lets it photosynthesise

question: What is the function of a muscle cell?
answer: to contract to move things
accept: it contracts so movement can happen

question: Why does a muscle cell contain many mitochondria?
answer: It needs to release lots of energy for movement
options: It needs to release lots of energy for movement
It needs to carry lots of oxygen
It needs to make lots of food by photosynthesis
It needs to absorb lots of water
distractors: carrying oxygen is the job of red blood cells, not muscle cells
muscle cells do not photosynthesise; that needs chloroplasts
absorbing water is the job of a root hair cell
misconceptions: mitochondria store energy rather than release it

question: A root hair cell has a long extension. Explain how this helps it absorb water.
answer: the long extension increases the surface area of the cell
a larger surface area increases the rate of diffusion, so more water is absorbed
accept: bigger surface area means faster diffusion of water

question: A root hair cell has no chloroplasts. Explain why.
answer: it is found underground
so it cannot photosynthesise and does not need chloroplasts
misconceptions: root hair cells are not plant cells
chloroplasts would help it absorb water
it has no chloroplasts because it has no nucleus

question: The function of a palisade cell is photosynthesis. Explain why it contains many more chloroplasts than other cells.
answer: chloroplasts are where photosynthesis happens
more chloroplasts absorb more light, so photosynthesis is faster
accept: more chloroplasts means more light trapped and faster photosynthesis

question: A red blood cell has an actual size of 0.008 mm. It is viewed at a magnification of 250. What is the size of the image?
answer: 2 mm
working: image size = actual size × magnification = 0.008 × 250 = 2 mm

question: What is the function of the nucleus in a cell?
answer: it controls the cell's activities
and contains the genetic material (DNA)
misconceptions: it is the brain of the cell

question: Which sub-cellular structure is found in plant cells but NOT in animal cells?
answer: Chloroplast
options: Nucleus
Chloroplast
Cell membrane
Mitochondrion
distractors: the nucleus is found in both animal and plant cells
the cell membrane is found in all cells
mitochondria are found in both animal and plant cells
misconceptions: only animal cells have a nucleus

question: Name the three extra sub-cellular structures a plant cell has that an animal cell does not.
answer: cell wall
permanent vacuole
chloroplasts

question: What is the function of the cell wall in a plant cell?
answer: it provides a rigid structure to the cell
helping it keep its shape / providing support
accept: strong and tough to keep shape
misconceptions: it controls what enters and leaves the cell
animal cells also have a cell wall

question: What is the function of the permanent vacuole in a plant cell?
answer: it stores water in the form of cell sap
which keeps the cell rigid
accept: filled with sap to keep the cell rigid
misconceptions: animal cells have the same permanent vacuole

question: What is the function of chloroplasts and why are they green?
answer: chloroplasts are where photosynthesis happens
they are green because they trap sunlight, giving leaves their green colour
accept: where the plant makes its own food

question: In which sub-cellular structure does respiration take place?
answer: the mitochondria (mitochondrion)

question: What process happens in the mitochondria, and what does it do?
answer: respiration happens in the mitochondria
respiration releases energy from food molecules

question: Which of these structures is found in BOTH animal and plant cells?
answer: Mitochondria
options: Cell wall
Chloroplast
Mitochondria
Permanent vacuole
distractors: the cell wall is only in plant (and bacterial) cells
chloroplasts are only in plant cells
the permanent vacuole is only in plant cells
misconceptions: animal cells have a cell wall
animal cells have a permanent vacuole

question: Why is it wrong to call the nucleus the 'brain' of the cell?
answer: it does not think
it just contains the instructions (genetic material) and controls the cell's activities
accept: brains are made of cells too
misconceptions: it is the brain because it controls everything
the nucleus makes decisions like a brain

question: A student looks at a cell under a microscope and sees a cell wall and chloroplasts. Is it an animal or plant cell, and how do you know?
answer: it is a plant cell
because a cell wall and chloroplasts are only found in plant cells, not animal cells
accept: plant cell - animal cells do not have a cell wall or chloroplasts
misconceptions: you can tell from the shape of the cell
all cells have a cell wall so you cannot tell

question: Why should you identify a cell type by its sub-cellular structures rather than its shape?
answer: cells can vary in shape and size
so shape is not reliable; only the structures present (e.g. cell wall, chloroplasts) tell you the type
accept: cells of the same type can have different shapes
shape varies but structures do not
misconceptions: a different shape means the diagram is a mistake
all plant cells are square and all animal cells are round
you can always tell from shape

question: Where in the cell is the genetic material (DNA) found in an animal cell?
answer: inside the nucleus

question: Which statement correctly compares an onion cell with a cheek cell viewed under the microscope?
answer: Onion cells tend to be squarer than cheek cells
options: Cheek cells tend to be squarer than onion cells
Onion cells tend to be squarer than cheek cells
Onion and cheek cells are always exactly the same shape
Only cheek cells contain a nucleus
distractors: this reverses the shapes; the onion (plant) cell is the squarer one
cells of different types do not have to be the same shape
both onion and cheek cells contain a nucleus
misconceptions: the shape of a cell is a mistake
only one of them has a nucleus

question: A cell is 0.05 mm wide and is magnified 200 times under a microscope. How wide does the image of the cell appear?
answer: 10 mm
working: image size = actual size × magnification = 0.05 × 200 = 10 mm

question: A plant cell has an actual width of 0.04 mm. Under a microscope its image appears 16 mm wide. What magnification was used?
Define diffusion.
answer: diffusion is the movement of a substance
from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration
accept: movement of particles from high to low concentration
spreading from where there is more to where there is less
misconceptions: movement from low to high concentration
movement that needs energy from the cell
particles only move until they stop

question: Explain how oxygen enters a cell by diffusion.
answer: there is a higher concentration of oxygen outside the cell than inside
so oxygen diffuses through the cell membrane from high to low concentration, into the cell
accept: oxygen moves down its concentration gradient into the cell through the membrane
misconceptions: oxygen is pushed in by the cell using energy
oxygen moves from low to high concentration

question: In which direction do particles move during diffusion?
answer: From high concentration to low concentration
options: From low concentration to high concentration
From high concentration to low concentration
Only from outside to inside the cell
Particles do not move during diffusion
distractors: this is the wrong way round; particles move down the gradient, not up it
diffusion can move particles either into or out of the cell, depending on where concentration is higher
during diffusion particles are constantly moving
misconceptions: particles move from low to high concentration
diffusion only ever moves substances into the cell
particles stop moving once spread out

question: What happens to the particles once diffusion is complete and the concentration is equal everywhere?
answer: the particles keep moving
but they stay evenly spread out, so there is no overall change in concentration
accept: particles do not stop, they remain evenly spread
they carry on moving but the spread stays even
misconceptions: the particles all move back to one side

question: Name the two factors covered at KS3 that increase the rate of diffusion.
answer: a larger surface area
a higher temperature

question: Explain why increasing the temperature increases the rate of diffusion.
answer: increasing the temperature gives the particles more energy
so the particles move around faster
meaning they spread out (diffuse) more quickly, increasing the rate of diffusion
accept: hotter particles have more energy and move faster, so diffuse quicker
misconceptions: temperature has no effect on diffusion

question: Explain why a cell with a wavy, folded surface has a faster rate of diffusion than a smooth cell.
answer: the wavy surface gives the cell a larger surface area
so there are more channels for particles to pass through
a larger surface area increases the rate of diffusion
accept: more folds means more surface area and more channels, so faster diffusion
misconceptions: a wavy surface has a smaller surface area
surface area does not affect diffusion

question: Cell C is found in a hot thermal vent and cell A is found in the cold Arctic. In which cell will diffusion happen faster, and why?
answer: diffusion will be faster in cell C (the thermal vent)
because the higher temperature gives the particles more energy so they move faster
accept: cell C because it is hotter and higher temperature increases the rate of diffusion
misconceptions: diffusion is faster in the cold Arctic cell
the Arctic cell has more particles

question: What does the phrase 'rate of diffusion' mean?
answer: how fast substances move / how fast diffusion happens
misconceptions: the amount of substance that diffuses in total

question: Carbon dioxide is a waste product made inside a cell. Explain why it leaves the cell by diffusion.
answer: there is a higher concentration of carbon dioxide inside the cell than outside
so it diffuses through the cell membrane from high to low concentration, out of the cell
accept: CO2 is more concentrated inside, so it moves out down the concentration gradient
misconceptions: carbon dioxide moves from low to high concentration
the cell pushes it out using energy
carbon dioxide enters the cell by diffusion

question: Through which part of the cell do substances diffuse to enter or leave?
answer: The cell membrane
options: The nucleus
The cell membrane
The cytoplasm
The ribosomes
distractors: the nucleus controls the cell, it is not the entry point for substances
the cytoplasm is where reactions happen, substances must first cross the membrane
ribosomes make proteins, they do not control what enters the cell
misconceptions: substances diffuse through the cell wall in all cells

question: Box B contains many particles packed closely together; box A contains only a few particles. Which box shows a high concentration, and which shows a low concentration?
answer: box B shows a high concentration
box A shows a low concentration
accept: B = high concentration, A = low concentration
misconceptions: both boxes have the same concentration

question: Why is diffusion especially important for a unicellular organism such as a bacterium?
answer: it is made of only one cell that is on its own
so it must rely on diffusion to obtain substances and remove waste, with no other cells to help it
accept: a single cell has no other cells to support it, so depends on diffusion
misconceptions: unicellular organisms do not use diffusion
many cells help the bacterium obtain substances
diffusion only happens in multicellular organisms

question: How does increasing a cell's surface area affect the rate of diffusion?
answer: The greater the surface area, the greater the rate of diffusion
options: A greater surface area slows diffusion down
A greater surface area has no effect on diffusion
The greater the surface area, the greater the rate of diffusion
A smaller surface area always speeds up diffusion
distractors: a larger surface area speeds diffusion up, it does not slow it down
surface area is one of the two KS3 factors that does affect the rate
this is the opposite; a smaller surface area gives slower diffusion
misconceptions: surface area has no effect on diffusion
a smaller surface area is faster

question: A root hair cell has a long, thin extension. Explain how this helps it absorb water by diffusion.
answer: the long extension increases the cell's surface area
and a larger surface area increases the rate of diffusion, so it absorbs water faster
accept: bigger surface area from the hair means more diffusion of water in
misconceptions: the shape has no effect on diffusion
the hair lets water in through chloroplasts

question: Name two substances that enter a cell and one that leaves a cell as waste, all by diffusion.
answer: enter: oxygen and food molecules (nutrients)
leave: carbon dioxide
accept: in: oxygen and food/nutrients; out: carbon dioxide
misconceptions: oxygen leaves the cell as waste
only one substance can diffuse at a time
